findings.description: IP spoofing: _client_key trusts the leftmost (client-supplied) X-Forwarded-For entry, so an attacker rotating forged XFF values gets a fresh bucket per request and fully bypasses the limiter (CWE-348/CWE-290).

Off-by-one: `len(hits) <= self.limit` allows and appends when already at the cap, letting each client make limit+1 requests per window instead of limit, weakening the throttling control (should be `<`) (CWE-799/CWE-770).

Unbounded memory DoS: _hits is a defaultdict keyed by attacker-controllable IP strings and sweep() is never invoked within this module, so spoofed-key floods grow the dict without bound (CWE-770/CWE-400).
check() reads self._hits[key] via defaultdict, materializing a bucket even for requests that will be denied, amplifying the memory-exhaustion vector from novel keys.
current_usage() looks read-only but `self._hits[key]` on a defaultdict inserts an empty bucket for any unseen key, so a monitoring/attacker call with novel keys leaks memory; use .get(key, []).

Race condition: the prune/len-check/append on hits has no locking, so concurrent check() calls for one key can both pass the limit and append, bypassing the limiter under load; sweep() mutating _hits during a concurrent check() key-insert can also raise 'dictionary changed size during iteration' (CWE-362).
hits.pop(0) is O(n) making the prune loop O(n^2) for a hot bucket (algorithmic-complexity DoS angle); a collections.deque gives O(1) popleft — perf detail deferred to the performance critic.
